coredata.dc:title: Preparation and application of carbon dots@cyanuric acid composites with aggregation-induced emission effect
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Jianxi
Zhou, Lianghua
Ye, Weihao
Zhang, Yan
Hu, Guangqi
Liu, Xiaotang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect can effectively solve the problem of weak fluorescence of solid CDs due to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect. A kind of carbon dots@cyanuric acid composites (CDs@CYA) with AIE effect was reported in this paper, which is prepared by one-step microwave-assisted method using low-toxicity and low-cost imidazole, urea and tartaric acid as precursors. The AIE effect of CDs@CYA is derived from the restriction of intramolecular motion of the imidazole and cyanuric acid (CYA) groups on the surface of CDs in the aggregation state and convert non-radiative energy into radiative energy, as is verified by control variable experiments. CDs@CYA aqueous solution was used in fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink, light conversion film (LCF), fluorescent probe and biological imaging. CDs@CYA powder was encapsulated on the blue chip with red phosphor by epoxy resin to obtain a white light-emitting diode (LED) device with a color coordinate of (0.33, 0.28), a color temperature of 5558 K and a color rendering index of 76.4.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Aggregation-induced emission effect
Green synthesis
Fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink
White LED devices

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangzhou (2023B03J1393),University Research Platforms and Projects by the Guangdong Provincial Department of Education (2021ZDZX4051), and PhD Development Enhancement Project of Guangdong Polytechnic Normal University (22GPNUZDJS35).

The work was supported by Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangzhou (2023B03J1393),University Research Platforms and Projects by the Guangdong Province Department of Education (2021ZDZX4051), and PhD Development Enhancement Project of Guangdong Polytechnic Normal University (22GPNUZDJS35).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Preparation of CDs@CYA. (b) Photographs of CDs@CYA aqueous solution and powder under sunlight and 365 nm ultraviolet (UV) lamp. (c) AIE effect mechanism of CDs@CYA.

TEM image of CDs@CYA-0 (a) and HR-TEM image of CDs in CYA crystal (b). (c) Diameters distribution of CDs in CYA. (d) XRD patterns of CDs@CYA-0 and standard cards of CYA, (e) FT-IR spectra of CDs@CYA-0, CDs-1 and CDs-2. (f) XPS spectra of CDs@CYA-0. High resolution XPS spectra and the corresponding fitting curves of C1s (g), N1s (h) and O1s (i) of CDs@CYA-0.

UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence excitation spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of aqueous solution (a) and powder (b) of CDs@CYA-0. Fluorescence emission spectra of aqueous solution (c) and powder (d) of CDs@CYA-0 at different excitation wavelengths. (e) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@CYA-0 in N, N-dimethylformamide, ethyl acetate, ethanol and water. (f) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solutions with different mass concentrations; (g) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solutions with varying ethanol ratios. Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@CYA-7 aqueous solutions with different mass concentrations (h) and CDs-1 solutions with different volume fraction (i).

Fluorescence decay curves of CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution (a) and powder (b). (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@CYA powders prepared with different mass ratios of imidazole, urea and tartaric acid. (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@CYA powders prepared with different volume of water and reaction time. (e) Solid-state fluorescence properties and AIE effect of CDs and CDs@CYA powders prepared with different mass ratios of imidazole, urea and tartaric acid.

(a) Temperature dependent fluorescence spectra of CDs@CYA-0 powder. (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solutions at different pH. (c) Fluorescence spectra of CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solutions at different concentration of NaCl. (d) Fluorescence spectra of the solutions configured with CDs@CYA-0 powder treated at different temperature.

Photographs of patterns placed on filter paper treated with CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution and untreated filter paper under sunlight and UV lamp, and photographs taken under UV lamp after drying. The filter paper strips treated with CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution are marked by red line. (b) Photographs of “S”, “C”, “A”, and “U” letter-shaped filter papers treated by different methods under UV lamp. (c) Emission spectra of LED-1, plant growth lamp, and commercial white LED. (d) The CIE coordinate diagram of LED-1, the inset is a photograph of LED-1. Photographs of a paper printed with different color fonts under sunlight (e) and LED-1 (f).

Photographs of Blank PU film and LCF under sunlight (a) and UV lamp (b). (c) Excitation and emission spectra of LCF. (d) Emission spectra of CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution containing different metal ions (d). (e) Normalized fluorescence intensity of the CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution in the presence of various metal ions.

Photographs of control group (a) and experimental group (b) of mung bean sprouts under sunlight. Photographs of control group (c) and experimental group (d) of mung bean sprouts under UV lamp. Fluorescence microscope images of cross-sections from the stems of control group (e) and experimental group (f) of mung bean sprouts.
Naming of CDs@CYA powders and CDs solution prepared by different mass ratios of imidazole, urea and tartaric acid, the volume of water and reaction time.

row.entry: Mass ratio of imidazole, urea and tartaric acid

The values of QY, τ, K
r and K
nr of CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution and powder.

head.ce:title: Preparation and application of carbon dots@cyanuric acid composites with aggregation-induced emission effect

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect can effectively solve the problem of weak fluorescence of solid CDs due to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect. A kind of carbon dots@cyanuric acid composites (CDs@CYA) with AIE effect was reported in this paper, which is prepared by one-step microwave-assisted method using low-toxicity and low-cost imidazole, urea and tartaric acid as precursors. The AIE effect of CDs@CYA is derived from the restriction of intramolecular motion of the imidazole and cyanuric acid (CYA) groups on the surface of CDs in the aggregation state and convert non-radiative energy into radiative energy, as is verified by control variable experiments. CDs@CYA aqueous solution was used in fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink, light conversion film (LCF), fluorescent probe and biological imaging. CDs@CYA powder was encapsulated on the blue chip with red phosphor by epoxy resin to obtain a white light-emitting diode (LED) device with a color coordinate of (0.33, 0.28), a color temperature of 5558 K and a color rendering index of 76.4.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: CDs as a new type of fluorescent nanomaterial [1], have a large number of advantages such as low-toxicity [2], strong biocompatibility [3], low-cost [4], diverse materials [5] and simple preparation [6]. The most prominent are their excellent hydrophilic [7] and fluorescence properties [8]. These properties herald huge development potential and broad application prospects for CDs [9]. Such as luminescent solar concentrator [10], optoelectronic devices [11,12], information encryption [13], anticounterfeiting [14] and biological imaging [15]. Although CDs have excellent properties, the fluorescence intensity of solid CDs is weakened by ACQ effect due to excessive resonance energy transfer between CDs [16], surface electron transition [17] and π-π stacking between cores of CDs [18].

The solid CDs are usually prepared by preparing CDs-based composites and CDs with AIE effect. The former involves dispersing CDs in homogeneous matrices such as lignin [19], noble metals [20] and silica [21] to inhibit the aggregation of CDs, so as to achieve the fluorescence of solid CDs. However, this method cannot really eliminate ACQ effect, which has an impact on the fluorescence properties of CDs-based composites [22]. The latter involves the preparation of CDs that restrict the intramolecular motion of groups on the surface of CDs in the aggregate state. When CDs with AIE effect reach the excited state, less energy is wasted on intramolecular motion and more energy is used to emit fluorescence, which can effectively eliminate the ACQ effect of CDs [23,24]. However, according to available reports, the main materials for the preparation of CDs with AIE effect were hazardous chemicals, such as piperazine [25], phthalic acid dianhydride [26] and dithiosalicylic acid [27], which are harmful to humans and the environment. Therefore, it is necessary to study the method of using low-toxicity and low-cost materials as precursors to prepare CDs composites with AIE effect.

This paper is the first to use a one-step microwave-assisted method to prepare CDs and CYA composite with AIE effect (carbon dots@cyanuric acid composites, CDs@CYA) (Fig. 1 a and b). Compared to using one-step hydrothermal method [28], two-step hydrothermal method [29–31], and two-step microwave-assisted method [32] for preparation CDs@CYA, the one-step microwave-assisted method has short reaction time, high reaction efficiency, and simple instrument requirements, while achieving the goal of simplifying the preparation process and conserving energy simultaneously. Compared with CDs-based composites using other matrices, such as lignin [19], noble metals [20] and silica [21], CDs@CYA not only can be dissolved in water to produce a repeatable fluorescent solution, but also exhibits the property that the fluorescence intensity increases with the degree of aggregation. Compared with other CDs with AIE effect using other precursors, such as piperazine [25], phthalic acid dianhydride [26] and dithiosalicylic acid [27], CDs@CYA uses low-toxicity and low-cost imidazole, urea and tartaric acid. The method used in this paper is more environmentally friendly and green.

In addition, it is meaningful adding an appropriate amount of imidazole as a precursor, as it not only increases fluorescence intensity of CDs@CYA can also synergizes with CYA to endow CDs@CYA with AIE effect (Fig. 1c).

The application of CDs@CYA in the fields of fluorescent anti-counterfeiting inks, white LED devices, LCF, fluorescent probe and biological imaging were explored based on the unique fluorescence properties of CDs@CYA.

Imidazole and FeCl3 were purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. Urea and KCL were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory. Tartaric acid and NaCl were purchased from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. Ethanol was purchased from Shanghai Titan Technology Co., LTD. Ethyl acetate was purchased from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., LTD. N, N-dimethylformamide was purchased from Shanghai Runjie Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. CaCl2 was purchased from Aladdin Chemicals. BaCl2 was purchased from Shantou Xihua Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. FeCl3 was purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without any purification. Epoxy resin glue A and epoxy curing agent B were purchased from Shenzhen Mingde New Material Technology Co., LTD. Red phosphor (model number: ZYP650G3) was purchased from Beijing Zhongcun Yuji Technology Co., LTD. Polyurethane (PU) was purchased from Shenzhen Jitian Chemical Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water was used throughout the study.

CDs@CYA powder was synthesized by a one-step microwave-assisted method according to the experimental conditions listed in Table 1 . For example, the synthesis method of CDs@CYA-0 was as follows: 1.00 g imidazole, 1.00 g urea and 0.10 g tartaric acid were dissolved in 10 mL of water in a 150 mL beaker with the assistance of ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. Then, the solution was heated in a microwave oven at 700 W for 5 min. After the reaction, the solution volatilized. 30 mL water was added to the beaker as it cooled down to room temperature. After stirring, the aqueous solution was centrifuged at 7500 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant was filtered through 0.22 μm pore-sized membrane filters and obtained yellow solution, dried at 80 °C in the oven for 24 h to get dark yellow powder. Then the powder was washed with ethanol for twice to the remove unreacted precursor, dried and get light yellow powder. The remaining samples were prepared according to the method outlined in Table 1. By measuring the fluorescence intensity of the samples, the optimal preparation conditions for CDs@CYA with the highest fluorescence intensity were determined.

In order to study the influence of the surface groups of CDs and the ratio of CYA on the fluorescence intensity, a CDs solution without CYA was prepared and purified by dialysis method. Taking CDs-1 for example, 1.00 g imidazole and 0.10 g tartaric acid were dissolved in 10 mL of water in a 150 mL beaker with the assistance of ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. The aqueous solution was then heated in a microwave oven at 700 W for 5 min 30 mL water was added to the beaker as it cooled down to room temperature. After stirring, the solution was centrifuged at 7500 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant was dialyzed with water in a dialysis bag (3500 Da) for purification and obtained yellow solution, then concentrated to 3 mL. CDs-1 solution was synthesized and stored at room temperature.

0.130 g CDs@CYA-0 powder and 0.003 g red phosphor were ground and mixed in 0.200 g epoxy resin glue A and 0.200 g epoxy curing agent B with rapid stirring. Then, the mixed phosphor glue was appropriately coated on the blue chip emitting 450 nm light and cured in the oven at 60 °C for 2 h. Finally, a white LED device (LED-1) was obtained.

0.003 g CDs@CYA-0 powder was dissolved in 1 mL water and treated with ultrasound for 5 min. Then, 1.500 g of PU solution was added and the solution was stirred at room temperature for 5 min. Finally, the LCF was produced by molding at 60 °C for 12 h.

CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution (1 g/L) was used as the blank solution. 3 mg CDs@CYA-0 powder was added to 3 mL 1 mM of different kinds of metal ions (Na+, K+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+ and Fe3+) aqueous solution, respectively. The mixed solutions were with the assistance of ultrasonic treatment for 5 min. In addition, the fluorescence spectra were collected at an excitation wavelength of 362 nm.

Mung bean seeds were selected (30 seeds for each group) and arranged in beakers. Water and CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution (1 g/L) were added into the above beakers, respectively, and cultured at room temperature for a week without light. Water for the control group and the CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution for the experimental groups were renewed every day.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) were recorded using a Thermo Scientific Talos F200S. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected using a Rigaku Ultima IV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were taken on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet IS10. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were performed on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured by a Shimadzu UV-2600i spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were measured by a Shimadzu RF-5301PC. The emission spectra, CIE coordinates, color temperature and color rendering index of the white LED device were measured by a SFIM-400 spectral scintillation illuminometer. Absolute quantum yield (QY) and time-resolved fluorescence spectra were measured by an Edinburgh FLS1000. Temperature dependent fluorescence spectra were measured by a Horiba Fluorolog-QM. Fluorescence microscope images were recorded using a Nikon Ti-s.

CDs@CYA-0 was prepared with imidazole, urea and tartaric acid by a one-step microwave-assisted method. The yield of CDs@CYA-0 after purification is 22.47 %. The TEM image of CDs@CYA-0 (Fig. 2 a) shows that the structure of CDs@CYA-0 is composed of CYA crystal and CDs and CDs are densely distributed in CYA crystals. Compared with dispersed in aqueous solution, CDs have a higher degree of aggregation in CYA crystal, which limits the motion of CDs surface groups and converts non-radiative energy into radiative energy, thereby enhancing the fluorescence intensity of CDs@CYA-0 powder. HR-TEM image (Fig. 2b) shows that the lattice spacing of CDs is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) face of graphite, indicating that CDs contain graphite-like structure [33]. As shown in Fig. 2c, in CYA crystals, the diameters of CDs are distributed between 4 and 16 nm, with an average diameter of 8.72 nm. The XRD pattern (Fig. 2d) shows that the diffraction peaks of CDs@CYA-0 match those of the standard CYA card, indicating that the CYA generated from urea in a one-step microwave-assisted reaction serves as the matrix of CDs@CYA-0 and is the main component of its crystal structure.

As shown in Fig. 2e, the FT-IR spectra of CDs-1 and CDs-2 are very different from that of CDs@CYA-0. The stretching vibration of N-C=N (1600 cm−1) indicates that the imidazole groups are successfully linked to the surface of CDs. Compared with CDs-1, CDs-2 showed a C-NH stretching vibration peak (1385 cm−1) after adding a small amount of urea, indicating that the addition of a small amount of urea allowed nitrogen-containing groups to enter the surface state of CDs. The N-H stretching vibration (3211 and 3084 cm−1), C=O stretching vibration (1700 cm−1), N-H bending vibration (1459 cm−1) and C-N stretching vibration (1417 cm−1) of cyclic amide indicated that CYA became the main component of the surface group of CDs@CYA-0. Several peaks between 1100 and 1700 cm−1 dominate characteristic stretching modes of C–N heterocycles, further suggesting the formation of covalent bond between CDs and CYA [34–36].

According to the results of XPS spectrum (Fig. 2f), the C1s spectrum (Fig. 2g) is separated into three peaks at 285, 286.9 and 289.8 eV, which are allocated to C-C/C=C, C=O and N-C=N bonds, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2h) is separated into two peaks at of 398.9 and 400.7 eV, which are allocated to C-N and C=N-C bonds, respectively. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2i) is separated into two peaks at 531.96 and 532.46 eV, which are allocated to the C=O and C-O bonds, respectively. By summarizing the corresponding fitting results of these deconvolution XPS spectra, it is obvious that the corresponding groups come from the surface of CDs, including the six-membered ring structure from CYA group, the C=C bond from imidazole group, and the C=O and C-O bonds from carboxyl group. It is proved that CYA and imidazole are bonded to the cores of CDs by C-N bond and CO-N bond to enter the surface states of CDs.

The UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution and powder are examined to assess their fluorescence properties. As shown in Fig. 3 a and b, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution has a peak at 253 nm, which is attributed to the π-π∗ transition of the C=C and n-π∗ transition of C=O/C=N bonds [28]. Compared with CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution, CDs@CYA-0 powder does not change the position of the strongest absorption peak, but shows an extended absorption range from 200 nm to 750 nm, indicating that the surface structure of the solid is conducive to absorbing energy and emitting stronger fluorescence. The strongest emission peak of CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution is at 442 nm under the excitation of 362 nm, and that of CDs@CYA-0 powder is at 530 nm under the excitation of 466 nm. The redshift of the excitation and emission during the conversion of CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution into powder is attributed to the increase of conjugated area and the decrease of energy band of CDs@CYA-0 [37]. The transformation of CDs@CYA-0 from aqueous solution to powder leads to the change of the surface state of CDs@CYA-0 due to aggregation [38]. In addition, the decrease of the distance between groups on the surface of CDs@CYA-0 may induce electron distribution and self-assembly of CDs@CYA-0, resulting in the formation of new molecular fluorophores and new emitting levels [39].

Fig. 3c, d represent the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution and powder at different excitation wavelengths, respectively. As the excitation wavelength increases from 310 nm to 430 nm, the position of fluorescence emission peak of CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution gradually redshifts. With the increase of the excitation wavelength from 410 nm to 490 nm, the position of fluorescence emission peak of CDs@CYA-0 powder increases from 501 nm to 540 nm. It indicates that both CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution and powder exhibit typical excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence behavior, which is usually attributed to both size effect and surface defect of CDs [40]. The behavior indicates that CDs are the fluorescence centers of CDs@CYA-0. As is shown in Fig. 3e, the position of fluorescence emission peak of CDs@CYA-0 gradually redshifts as the solvent polarity decreases, because of the abundant hydroxyl, amino, and carbonyl groups on the surfaces of the CDs, which form different hydrogen bonds with different solvents [41].

Fig. 3f, g shows the fluorescence emission spectra of aqueous solutions with different mass concentrations of CDs@CYA-0 and CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solutions with varying ethanol ratios. As the mass concentration increases, the emission peak of the CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution gradually rises, indicating that the aggregation degree of CDs@CYA-0 increases with increasing mass concentration. This results in the intramolecular motion of imidazole and cyanuric acid groups on the surface of CDs@CYA-0 being restricted, thereby converting the non-radiative energy intended for intramolecular motion into radiative energy, leading to stronger fluorescence emission.

Since CDs@CYA-0 is soluble in water but insoluble in ethanol, the aggregation state of CDs@CYA-0 in the mixed solution increases as the proportion of ethanol increases [25]. When CDs@CYA-0 in aqueous solutions with different proportions of ethanol, the fluorescence emission peak intensity increases with the increase of ethanol proportion, indicating that the fluorescence intensity of CDs@CYA-0 in the mixed solution increases with the degree of aggregation, which is consistent with the AIE effect.

As shown in Fig. 3h, when imidazole is not added, there is no imidazole group in the surface state of CDs@CYA-7. When the mass concentration of CDs@CYA-7 increased from 0.5 g/L to 1.5 g/L, CDs@CYA-7 always in a dispersed state in water, and the fluorescence intensity of CDs@CYA-7 aqueous solution increases with the increase of mass concentration. When the mass concentration of CDs@CYA-7 is greater than 1.5 g/L, it will cause aggregation of CDs@CYA-7 in water, and the fluorescence intensity of CDs@CYA-7 aqueous solution decreases with the increase of the mass concentration of CDs@CYA-7 due to the ACQ effect. In the synthesis of CDs system, a similar phenomenon occurs when urea is not added, as shown in Fig. 3i. There is no CYA group on the surface state of CDs-1 when urea is not added, and the powder cannot be formed due to the absence of CYA matrix. When the volume fraction of CDs-1 is greater than 50 %, it will cause aggregation of CDs-1 in water, and the fluorescence intensity of CDs-1 aqueous solution decreases with the increase of the volume fraction of CDs-1 due to ACQ effect. It is found that the lack of imidazole or urea as precursors would lead to the absence of AIE effect. Combined with FT-IR (Fig. 2e) and XPS (Fig. 2f–i) spectra analysis of CDs@CYA-0, the CYA generated by urea reaction is connected to the surface of CDs through C-N bonds and hydrogen bonds [31,42], and imidazole is also connected to the surface of CDs through amide bonds. Therefore, the AIE effect of CDs@CYA-0 is related to the synergistic effect of imidazole and CYA groups on the surface of CDs.

As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, the average lifetimes of CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution and powder are 6.97 and 7.13 ns under the excitation of 342 nm and 448 nm, respectively. Measured by Edinburgh FLS1000, the QY of CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution and powder are 12.0 % and 32.9 %, respectively. Then, the radiative rate constants (K
r ) and non-radiative rate constants (K
nr ) of CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution and powder can be calculated according to the following equations [43]:
(1)
K r
= Q Y / τ
(2)
K
n r
=
(
1 − Q Y
)
/ τ

The values of QY, τ, K
r and K
nr of CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution and powder are shown in Table 2 . The higher K
r and lower K
nr of CDs@CYA-0 powder are well in accordance with the AIE effect.

As shown in Fig. 4c and d, the optimal synthesis conditions for CDs@CYA-0 are a mass ratio of 10:10:1 of imidazole, urea, and tartaric acid, a volume of 10 mL of water, and a reaction time of 5 min. Interestingly, as shown in Fig. 4e, when the mass ratio of imidazole, urea and tartaric acid is 0:10:1, CDs-3 can only obtain viscous brown liquid after drying and cannot form powder. However, when the mass ratio is 0:20:1 and 10:10:1, CDs@CYA-7 and CDs@CYA-0 can form powder, but the fluorescence intensity of CDs@CYA-7 is much lower than that of CDs@CYA-0, and CDs@CYA-7 does not have AIE effect. Combination the analysis results of FT-IR and XPS spectra of CDs@CYA-0, it is speculated that the formation of CDs@CYA-0 powder is related to the matrix CYA formed by the condensation of urea at high temperature in microwave-assisted reaction, and the fluorescence intensity and AIE effect of CDs@CYA-0 are affected by the synergistic effect of imidazole and CYA groups in the surface state of CDs. When the concentration of urea is low, the amino group of urea undergoes amidation reaction with the carboxyl group of tartaric acid, becoming a functional group on the surface of CDs. Only a very small amount of urea undergoes high-temperature condensation reaction to form the matrix CYA, resulting in CDs-3 unable to form powder. When the concentration of urea is high, urea undergoes acylation reaction with tartaric acid, and excess urea undergo high-temperature condensation reaction to form matrix CYA, which is connected to the surface of CDs in the form of hydrogen bonds and covalent bonds. Therefore, CDs@CYA-7 can form a powder. At this point, only CYA groups are present on the surface of CDs, resulting in CDs@CYA-7 exhibit fluorescence intensity is weak and does not exhibit AIE effect.

When the mass ratio of imidazole, urea and tartaric acid is 10:10:1, CDs@CYA-0 has strong fluorescence intensity and AIE effect, which is attributed to the addition of imidazole causing the carboxyl group of tartaric acid to preferentially undergo amidation reaction with the N-3 of imidazole, inhibiting the amidation reaction of urea with tartaric acid. The urea that has not reacted with tartaric acid undergoes high-temperature condensation reaction to generate a large amount of CYA. The surface structure of CDs composed of imidazole and CYA groups is conducive to limiting the intramolecular motion of the groups in the aggregated state, converting non-radiative energy into radiative energy, thereby enhancing the fluorescence properties of CDs@CYA-0 powder.

The internal quantum efficiency, maximum output power, reliability and lifetime of LEDs strongly depend on the heat at the semiconductor's p–n junction, and the junction temperature of LED chips under normal operation does not exceed 150 °C [44]. Therefore, measuring the temperature dependent fluorescence spectra of CDs@CYA-0 powder is necessary. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@CYA-0 powder decreases with the increase of temperature. The maximum fluorescence intensity of CDs@CYA-0 powder at 333 K, 363 K, 393 K and 423 K are 93.8 %, 86.9 %, 82.2 % and 73.6 % of that at 303 K, respectively. It can be seen that the CDs@CYA-0 powder can maintain good fluorescence performance at the working temperature of the LED chip.

Fluorescent anti-counterfeiting inks and fluorescent probes will be used in a variety of detection environments. Therefore, it is significant to measure the fluorescence intensity of CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution at different pH and salinity. As shown in Fig. 5b, CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution has the strongest fluorescence intensity at pH = 9, and shows relatively good fluorescence intensity in alkaline environment. As shown in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution in different concentrations of NaCl has little change, indicating that CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution has favorable stability in different salinity. In addition, measuring fluorescence spectra of the aqueous solutions of CDs@CYA-0 treated with different temperature can explore the thermal stability of CDs@CYA-0. The fluorescence spectra of solutions configured with CDs@CYA-0 powder treated at different temperatures (Fig. 5d) shows that the fluorescence intensity of solutions configured with CDs@CYA-0 powder treated at no more than 423 K has little change, and has a slight weakening at 473 K, showing that CDs@CYA-0 has good thermal stability.

The filter strips treated with CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution were arranged in a pattern of “2024” (indicated by the red line in Fig. 6 a) and then untreated filter paper strips (white paper strips in Fig. 6a) are placed to form the pattern of “8888”. Under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp, a blue-green fluorescent pattern of “2024” is displayed in the “8888” pattern, which is the encrypted fluorescence number. After drying, the filter paper treated with CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution can emit stronger fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 6b, the filter papers with letter shapes of “S”, “C”, “A”, and “U” treated with CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution emit blue-green fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp. The fluorescence is temporarily quenched when the filter papers with letter shapes of “C” and “U” are treated with KOH solution. After drying the filter paper strip, all letters can emit fluorescence, while the blue fluorescence emitted by “C” and “U” is weak. Thus, we can conclude that the “S” and “A” emitting strong blue-green fluorescence are encrypted information for the first time, while the “C” and “U” emitting weaker blue fluorescence are eliminated information. It indicates that CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution has a promising application prospect in the field of fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink.

As shown in Fig. 6c, the mixed powder of CDs@CYA-0 and red phosphor was encapsulated on a blue chip using epoxy resin. The emission peaks of LED-1 obtained after solidification are at 450 nm, 530 nm, and 650 nm when powered on belong to the blue chip, CDs@CYA-0 powder and red phosphor, respectively. Compared with commercial white LEDs that lack red light emission, LED-1 is closer to natural light and exhibits better color rendering. Compared to commercially available red and blue chips plant growth lamps with narrowband emission, LED-1 has the advantage of full-spectrum broadband emission and thus has potential application in the field of plant growth lamps. According to the CIE diagram of LED-1 (Fig. 6d), the color coordinates of LED-1 are (0.33, 0.28), the color temperature is 5558 K, and the color rendering index is 76.4.

Exposure of A4 paper with different color fonts to sunlight (Fig. 6e) and LED-1 device (Fig. 6f) for photo comparison showed that bright and full colors could be achieved under LED-1 illumination. After measurement, the color coordinates of commercial white LED devices are (0.33, 0.34), with a color temperature of 5606 K and a color rendering index of 73.1, which is close to LED-1, indicating that CDs@CYA-0 has potential application in the field of white LED devices.

As shown in Fig. 7 a and b, LCF has almost no difference from blank PU film under sunlight, but emits bright blue light under UV lamp, indicating that CDs@CYA-0 is uniformly dispersed in the PU film and retains fluorescence properties. Excitation and emission spectra of LCF (Fig. 7c) show that the strongest emission peak is at 419 nm under the excitation of 365 nm, indicating that LCF can be applied to anti-counterfeiting.

As shown in Fig. 7d, e, Fe3+ can selectively quenches the fluorescence of CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution, while other ions have little effect on the fluorescence of CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution, which shows that CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution has application potential in fluorescent probe for Fe3+.

The mung bean seeds are grown in deionized water as the control group and in CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution as experimental group. After cultivation at room temperature for a week, the bean seeds grow into bean sprouts. There is no significant difference between the control group (Fig. 8 a) and the experimental group (Fig. 8b) under sunlight, indicating that CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution has no effect on the growth of bean sprouts in the short term. However, compared with the control group (Fig. 8b), the experimental group (Fig. 8d) emit blue fluorescence at stem near the root and green fluorescence at the surface of leaves under UV lamp, which verifies that CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution could be absorbed by bean sprouts. Fluorescence microscope images of cross-sections from the stem of control group (Fig. 8e) and experimental group (Fig. 8f) show that the fluorescence signals of CDs@CYA-0 aqueous solution exist in the side of cross-section of stem of the experimental group, while the control group has no fluorescence signal, indicating that CDs@CYA-0 has potential application significance in biological imaging.

In summary, CDs@CYA with AIE effect were prepared by one-step microwave-assisted method. Its aqueous solution and powder emit blue light under the excitation of UV and green light under the excitation of blue light, respectively, and both exhibit typical excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence behavior. The increase of the mass concentration of CDs@CYA in aqueous solution and the increase of the proportion of ethanol in ethanol-aqueous solution both lead to increase the fluorescence intensity of CDs@CYA, showing AIE effect. Through optimization experiments, it was found that CDs@CYA has the strongest fluorescence intensity when the mass ratio of imidazole, urea and tartaric acid is 10:10:1, the volume of water was 10 mL, and the reaction time was 5 min. The AIE effect of CDs@CYA is related to imidazole and CYA groups, and it is speculated that the surface structure of CDs composed of imidazole and CYA groups is conducive to limiting the intramolecular motion of groups in the aggregation state, converting non-radiative energy into radiative energy, thereby increasing the fluorescence intensity of CDs@CYA powder. The water solubility and unique fluorescence properties of CDs@CYA make it promising for application in anti-counterfeiting fluorescent inks, white LED devices, LCF, fluorescent probe and biological imaging.

Jianxi Xie: Writing – original draft, Validation, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Lianghua Zhou: Investigation. Weihao Ye: Investigation. Yan Zhang: Investigation. Guangqi Hu: Resources, Investigation. Xiaotang Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangzhou (2023B03J1393),University Research Platforms and Projects by the Guangdong Province Department of Education (2021ZDZX4051), and PhD Development Enhancement Project of Guangdong Polytechnic Normal University (22GPNUZDJS35).
